text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 14 December 2020 

Dr Amelia Carter  
St Thomas’ Hospital  
Department of Neurology, Westminster Bridge Road, London SE1 7EH  

Dear Dr Amelia Carter,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 17-03-1982, Hospital No: H392754 NHS No. 9482051736  
22 Brookfield Avenue, London, N10 1LD 

Diagnoses: 

Focal epilepsy with nocturnal motor seizures (onset 2020)  
Type 2 diabetes mellitus managed in primary care 

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 500 mg bd  
Metformin 1 g bd 

Plan: 

- Continue levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily  
- Safety advice provided regarding sleep hygiene, alcohol moderation, and adherence  
- Liaise with GP regarding ongoing Type 2 diabetes management; no antiepileptic–metformin interactions anticipated  
- Arrange MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and EEG to further characterise focal onset  
- Follow up in neurology clinic in 6 months, sooner if further events occur 

I reviewed Michael Turner in my clinic today regarding his new-onset seizures. He has background Type 2 diabetes managed in primary care with metformin and regular HbA1c monitoring; he reports stable glucose readings and no recent hypoglycaemia.

His initial event was in June 2020 in Germany, arising from sleep. He awoke with jerking of the left arm and facial twitching. A second event occurred in Italy the following December 2020, once more during the night, lasting four minutes with similar semiology. He recalls post-event confusion but no tongue biting or urinary incontinence. There is no history of head injury, CNS infection, or family history of epilepsy. Alcohol intake is modest, and he denies recreational drug use. Sleep has occasionally been curtailed due to travel.

The semiology is suggestive of focal motor seizures with possible spread, consistent with focal epilepsy. Neurological examination today is normal. Routine blood tests arranged by his GP in November 2020 (FBC, U&Es, LFTs, glucose profile) were unremarkable aside from known hyperglycaemia consistent with Type 2 diabetes. We discussed the relationship between sleep deprivation and seizure threshold, and he agrees to prioritise regular sleep and medication adherence.

He has commenced levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily with good tolerability and no mood change or sedation reported. I advised that driving restrictions apply in accordance with DVLA guidance for seizures; he understands the requirements. We will proceed to MRI brain with epilepsy protocol and EEG to evaluate for a focal cortical source, and I will review results at his follow-up. I am happy to provide interim advice should there be any further events or adverse effects.

With best wishes, 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes two seizure events: one in June 2020 and another in December 2020. Clinic date is 14 December 2020. The second event occurred in December 2020; timing relative to clinic suggests roughly 6 months between the two events. No other events are reported. Therefore, the best explicit inference is 2 seizures over a 12-month period (June to December spans within the same year, but since it's June and December, that implies approximately 6 months; however, the letter gives only two discrete events without specifying a precise ongoing rate beyond those two). Given the allowed formats and the need to express frequency, the most defensible normalization is 2 per year, but since the two events are within the same calendar year and six months apart, 2 per 6 months is mathematically equivalent yet the formats only allow day/week/month/year. Since the exact span is not explicitly stated as six months, choosing per year is safer. However, the letter clearly states one in June and one in December—exactly 6 months apart—so reporting 2 per 6 months is precise and allowed. Selecting the highest frequency per rule is not needed as only one type is described.
